2.label: Issue a staged, threshold-based school closure protocol
2.summary: Publish a written pressure threshold and a staged closure protocol for the three south-zone schools.
2.rationale: Gives the superintendent a defensible, on-the-record rule instead of a guess, and blunts the concealment narrative.
2.recommendation: Adopt a written pressure floor below which the three south-zone schools close, with a staged reopening test.
expected_benefits: Replaces a guess with a defensible, published threshold.
Parents see on-the-record action; trust steadies.
expected_harms: A precautionary closure spends budget and disrupts families even if the water proves fine.
Publishing a closure rule signals the crisis is real, raising media pressure.
operational_steps: Set the sanitation and pressure measurements that trigger each closure stage.
Publish transportation, meal, and remote-learning arrangements with the threshold.
Require a documented verification check before reopening each building.

3.label: Document priority water allocation for the hospital
3.summary: Formally allocate priority pressurized supply to dialysis and sterilization, with tanker resupply as backup.
3.rationale: Protects the most acute clinical need and creates a clean record before a harm event can occur.
3.recommendation: Issue documented priority allocation for clinical water and pre-stage tanker resupply for dialysis and sterilization.
expected_benefits: Clinical operations are protected before a cascade begins.
A documented allocation reduces liability exposure.
expected_harms: Diverting pressurized supply thins the general system margin.
A visible priority for the hospital can trigger a fairness dispute with residents.
operational_steps: Document the dialysis and sterilization volumes that receive priority supply.
Stage tanker backup and define the pressure reading that triggers delivery.
Brief resident representatives on the clinical basis and limits of the allocation.

7.label: Offer a compensation framework for mandatory closures
7.summary: Pair enforceable conservation restrictions with a limited compensation framework for affected businesses.
7.rationale: Trades fiscal capacity for enforceability, defusing the injunction threat without conceding the public-health line.
7.recommendation: Offer a capped compensation framework tied to compliance so restrictions hold up against an injunction.
expected_benefits: Keeps conservation restrictions enforceable; order improves.
Removes the coalition's strongest grounds for an injunction.
expected_harms: Compensation draws down already-thin budget capacity.
A framework can be read as admitting fault and invite further claims.
operational_steps: Define eligible closure losses, documentation requirements, and a capped appropriation.
Tie compensation eligibility to compliance with the conservation order.
Publish the framework and appeal path before enforcing the next restriction cycle.

4.label: Stage a competitive emergency procurement
4.summary: Qualify a second firm and split the emergency scope so the sole-source leverage stops setting the town's terms.
4.rationale: Slower and costlier this quarter, but it is the only lever that actually reduces structural dependency instead of renting it back one change order at a time.
4.title: Competitive emergency procurement
4.recommendation: Open an expedited qualification for a second certified firm and split the emergency scope, accepting a slower repair sequence to break sole-source leverage.
expected_benefits: Structural dependency on the sole-source firm actually falls.
Future change orders price against a real alternative, not a bluff.
expected_harms: The repair sequence slows while the second firm qualifies; water security slips.
Marquotte reads a competitive posture as a declaration of hostilities.
Expedited qualification invites a procurement-protest exposure.
operational_steps: Issue an expedited qualification notice scoped to the failing valve class.
Split the emergency scope so no single firm controls the critical path.
Document the sole-source risk record supporting the expedited process.

5.label: Publish a rotating load-shedding protocol
5.summary: Adopt a published, rotating load-management schedule with hospital and water operations exempted as critical loads.
5.rationale: The heat event is eating grid margin the water response depends on. A scheduled, owned outage beats an unplanned one that takes the pumps and the desk down with it.
5.title: Rotating load-shedding protocol
5.recommendation: Publish a rotating outage schedule with critical-load exemptions before the grid picks its own outage times.
expected_benefits: Restores operating margin the pumping and treatment response depends on.
Puts outages on a schedule the town owns instead of one the grid imposes.
Exempts hospital and water operations as documented critical loads.
expected_harms: Visible rationing reads as the crisis widening beyond water.
Scheduled outages strain compliance already stretched by conservation orders.
operational_steps: Designate hospital, treatment, and pumping feeders as exempt critical loads.
Publish the rotation calendar and a single point of contact for exemption claims.
Coordinate outage windows with the water authority's pumping schedule.
Brief the media desk before the first scheduled window, not after.
